title: Congee with Century Egg

seo.title: Authentic Century Egg Congee Recipe | Easy Chinese Breakfast

description: Congee with Century Egg, also known as Pidan Congee, is a quintessential Chinese comfort food, perfect for breakfast or a light meal. This creamy rice porridge, simmered to perfection, features the unique savory taste of preserved century eggs and tender pork. It's a deeply satisfying and nourishing dish that brings a taste of traditional Chinese home cooking to your table.

instructions.title: Prepare the Rice
instructions.text: Rinse the jasmine rice thoroughly under cold running water until the water runs clear. Drain well.

instructions.title: Prepare Pork and Century Eggs
instructions.text: Thinly slice or shred the lean pork tenderloin. Peel the century eggs and dice them into small, bite-sized pieces. Julienne the fresh ginger and chop the green onions, keeping a small portion of ginger and green onions aside for garnish.

instructions.title: Start the Congee Base
instructions.text: In a large heavy-bottomed pot, combine the rinsed rice with 8-10 cups of water or chicken broth. Bring the mixture to a rolling boil over high heat, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking.

instructions.title: Initial Simmer
instructions.text: Once boiling, immediately reduce the heat to low. Cover the pot, leaving a small gap for steam to escape, and let it simmer gently for 15 minutes. The rice grains will start to soften.

instructions.title: Add Flavorings
instructions.text: Stir in the prepared pork, diced century eggs, and most of the julienned ginger into the simmering congee. Continue to simmer, stirring occasionally.

instructions.title: Final Simmer and Season
instructions.text: Continue to simmer the congee for another 10 minutes, or until the rice grains have fully broken down and the congee has reached a thick, creamy consistency. Season generously with salt and white pepper to taste.

instructions.title: Serve and Garnish
instructions.text: Ladle the hot Century Egg Congee into individual serving bowls. Drizzle each bowl with a few drops of sesame oil and garnish with the reserved chopped green onions and julienned ginger. Serve immediately for a comforting meal.

tips: For an extra creamy congee, you can freeze the rinsed rice overnight before cooking. The freezing process breaks down the rice grains, allowing them to cook faster and achieve a silkier texture.
If you prefer a richer flavor, use chicken broth instead of water for the congee base. You can also add a tablespoon of dried scallops (rehydrated and shredded) for umami depth.

Congee tends to thicken significantly as it cools. When reheating, add a splash of hot water or broth and stir well to restore its desired consistency. Store leftovers in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 2-3 days.
article:
Embrace the ultimate comfort food for your morning routine with a classic Chinese breakfast that warms the soul and tantalizes the taste buds: Congee with Century Egg. This creamy, savory rice porridge, often enriched with lean pork and the uniquely flavorful century egg, is a staple across Chinese households. More than just a meal, it's an experience – a gentle start to the day that's both nourishing and deeply satisfying. If you're looking to explore authentic Chinese breakfast recipes, diving into the world of Century Egg Congee is an absolute must. Its distinctive aroma and complex flavors make it a beloved dish, offering a perfect blend of tradition and culinary adventure right in your own kitchen.

## What is Century Egg Congee?

At its heart, congee (or jook) is a simple rice porridge, a cornerstone of Asian cuisine. It's made by boiling rice in a large amount of water or broth until the grains break down into a thick, creamy consistency. This humble base serves as a canvas for countless additions, from meats and seafood to vegetables and seasonings. What elevates this particular dish to iconic status is the inclusion of the century egg, known in Chinese as *pidan*.

A century egg is a preserved duck, chicken, or quail egg that has undergone a fascinating transformation. Cured in a mixture of clay, ash, salt, quicklime, and rice hulls for several weeks to months, its albumen turns into a translucent, dark brown jelly, while the yolk becomes a creamy, dark green or gray paste. The preservation process imparts a unique umami flavor, often described as pungent, earthy, and slightly ammonia-like, yet surprisingly mild and delicious when paired correctly. In Century Egg Congee, the century egg is usually diced and added towards the end of cooking, allowing its distinct taste and texture to meld beautifully with the silky porridge and often, tender slivers of marinated pork. This combination creates a rich, savory, and incredibly comforting dish that defines a classic Chinese breakfast.

## Ingredients for Century Egg Congee

Crafting the perfect Century Egg Congee starts with selecting quality ingredients. Here's what you'll need for this hearty breakfast:

*   **Rice:** 1 cup long-grain jasmine rice or short-grain rice. Jasmine rice is preferred for its fragrance and ability to break down into a creamy texture.
*   **Water or Broth:** 8-10 cups of water, chicken broth, or pork broth. Broth significantly enhances the flavor.
*   **Century Eggs:** 2-3 century eggs, peeled and diced.
*   **Lean Pork (Optional but Recommended):** 4-6 ounces lean pork loin or shoulder, thinly sliced or shredded.
*   **Ginger:** 1-inch piece, peeled and julienned or finely minced.
*   **Green Onions:** 2 stalks, chopped for garnish.
*   **Salt:** To taste.
*   **White Pepper:** To taste.
*   **Sesame Oil:** A drizzle for finishing.
*   **Marinade for Pork (if using):** 1 tsp soy sauce, 1 tsp cornstarch, 1 tsp sesame oil, a pinch of white pepper.

## Step-by-Step Instructions to Make Congee

Making a delicious bowl of Congee with Century Egg is a straightforward process, though it requires a bit of patience.

1.  **Prepare the Rice:** Rinse the rice thoroughly under cold running water until the water runs clear. This removes excess starch and prevents the congee from becoming too gummy. For a faster cooking time, you can soak the rice in water for 30 minutes to an hour beforehand.
2.  **Marinate the Pork (if using):** If using pork, combine the thinly sliced or shredded pork with soy sauce, cornstarch, sesame oil, and white pepper. Mix well and set aside to marinate for at least 15 minutes. The cornstarch helps tenderize the pork and keeps it moist.
3.  **Start the Congee Base:** In a large pot or Dutch oven, bring 8-10 cups of water or broth to a rolling boil. Once boiling, add the rinsed rice. Reduce the heat to low, cover, and let it simmer gently.
4.  **Simmer and Stir:** Cook the congee for 1 to 1.5 hours, stirring occasionally (every 15-20 minutes) to prevent the rice from sticking to the bottom of the pot. As it cooks, the rice grains will break down and the congee will thicken to a creamy, porridge-like consistency. If it becomes too thick, add a little more hot water or broth.
5.  **Add Pork and Ginger:** Once the congee has reached your desired consistency, add the marinated pork and most of the julienned ginger. Stir gently to separate the pork slices and cook for another 5-7 minutes, or until the pork is cooked through.
6.  **Incorporate Century Eggs:** Add the diced century eggs to the congee. Stir gently and cook for just 2-3 minutes to warm them through. You don't want to overcook the century eggs, as this can diminish their unique texture.
7.  **Season and Serve:** Season the Century Egg Congee with salt and white pepper to taste. Ladle the hot congee into bowls. Garnish generously with chopped green onions, the remaining julienned ginger, and a drizzle of sesame oil. Serve immediately.

## Pro Tips for the Best Century Egg Congee

Achieving that perfect, restaurant-quality Century Egg Congee at home is easier with a few expert tricks:

*   **Rice Quality Matters:** While any long-grain rice will work, aged jasmine rice or even a short-grain sushi rice can give you a creamier texture. Some chefs even freeze the rice overnight after rinsing, as the freezing process helps the grains break down faster when cooked.
*   **Broth is Key:** Using chicken or pork broth instead of plain water will significantly deepen the flavor profile of your congee. If you're using plain water, consider adding a bouillon cube or some dried scallops for an umami boost.
*   **Constant Stirring:** While you don't need to stir continuously, regular stirring, especially in the last 30 minutes of cooking, is crucial. It prevents sticking and helps release starch, contributing to that coveted silky-smooth texture.
*   **Don't Overcook Century Eggs:** Add the century eggs towards the end. Their unique flavor and texture are best preserved when they are gently warmed through rather than boiled for a long period.
*   **Clean Cut for Century Eggs:** To prevent the century egg yolk from sticking to your knife, lightly oil your knife before dicing, or rinse it with water between cuts.
*   **Pressure Cooker or Instant Pot:** For a much faster cooking time (around 20-30 minutes), use a pressure cooker or Instant Pot. Follow the manufacturer's instructions for rice porridge.

## Serving Suggestions for Congee

Congee is incredibly versatile and can be enjoyed in many ways. While a bowl of Century Egg Congee is a complete meal in itself, it’s often accompanied by various side dishes and condiments to enhance the experience:

*   **Youtiao (Chinese Fried Dough Sticks):** These crispy, savory dough sticks are a classic pairing, perfect for dipping into the hot congee.
*   **Soy Sauce and Chili Oil:** A dash of light soy sauce can add extra savoriness, while a drizzle of chili oil provides a welcome kick for those who enjoy a bit of spice.
*   **Pickled Vegetables:** Fermented or pickled vegetables, like pickled radish or mustard greens, offer a tangy contrast that cuts through the richness of the congee.
*   **Roasted Peanuts:** A sprinkle of crunchy roasted peanuts adds a delightful textural contrast.
*   **White Pepper:** Always have extra white pepper on hand for those who like an extra aromatic zing.

This comforting dish is traditionally a breakfast staple but also makes a fantastic light lunch, dinner, or a soothing meal when you're feeling under the weather.

## Variations and Substitutions

The beauty of congee lies in its adaptability. While Century Egg Congee is a classic, don't hesitate to experiment:

*   **Different Proteins:** Instead of pork, you can use shredded chicken (often made with leftover roast chicken), thinly sliced fish fillets (like cod or tilapia), or even duck. Cook them in the same way as the pork, adding them towards the end.
*   **Vegetarian Options:** Omit the pork and use vegetable broth. You can add finely diced mushrooms (shiitake or oyster), carrots, or spinach for added nutrients and flavor. Tofu or rehydrated dried shiitake mushrooms can also add a nice texture and umami.
*   **Flavor Boosters:** Try adding a few dried scallops (soaked and shredded) to the congee base for an intense umami flavor. A splash of Shaoxing wine can also be added with the pork for extra depth.
*   **Quick Congee:** If you're short on time, use leftover cooked rice. Simply add it to boiling broth and simmer, stirring frequently, until it breaks down into a porridge (this will take less time than cooking from raw rice). Some instant congee mixes are also available for ultra-fast preparation, though homemade is always superior.

Whether you stick to the traditional Century Egg Congee recipe or venture into creative variations, this humble rice porridge offers a world of comforting flavors and culinary possibilities. It’s a dish that truly embodies the warmth and richness of Chinese home cooking.
